I received this same request in [jaraco.timing #1](https://github.com/jaraco/jaraco.timing/pull/1). I still have the reservations I mentioned there. I also have the reservation that if I accept this change here, I probably need to accept it across the dozens of projects that use it.

This request and the sister one is signaling to me that there's a defect here, but I think the defect lies with setuptools and/or pytest-runner not supporting environment markers. If environment markers were supported in `tests_require`, this platform selection could be made earlier, on `backports.unittest_mock`, but still be declarative. Given the state of the art, however, I lean toward always requiring backports.unittest_mock.

I'm willing to be persuaded otherwise. Given the concerns I outline above, what's your take? What is the impact of requiring the backport even on Python 3.3+?

After investigating the connections between ipdb and IPython a bit, I believe the best solution is to stop using ipdb and use the stand-alone debugger that comes with IPython. Documentation on this subject is extremely poor, but by replacing all calls to
```
import ipdb; ipdb.set_trace()
```
with
```
import IPython; IPython.terminal.debugger.TerminalPdb().set_trace()
```
you get all the functionality you are used to from ipdb. It seems that ipdb has always been just a very thin wrapper into IPython and the ipdb project itself seems obsolete to as of IPython 5.0.

Ok, I understand better now.

> Something similar to this should be done by the install script of a .deb/.rpm package (and I guess that complete_deploy) could be directly invoked by it, though I presume that packaging policies might prevent it

Yeah, that doesn't jibe well with packaging policies. In general it would be better if `python setup.py install` installed everything, but everything outside of the Python module being installed (e.g files in config files `/etc`, data in `/usr/share`, ...) is always such a pain to handle. The joys of Python packaging... 😞

In addition, `/etc/bash_completion.d/`, being under `/etc`, is for the local admin to add stuff. Distro packages should install Bash completion files in `/usr/share/bash-completion/completions/` instead. (I suspect the same is true for other shells, but I'll need to investigate what the right location would be for them)

At the very least, I think I'm going to do the following:

* get the Fedora package to install the completion files (just running the `/usr/bin/install` command on those files;
* remove the `/usr/lib/pythonX.Y/site-packages/pew/shell_config/complete_deploy` from the package, because it is useless once the completion files are in place;

I'm not sure what would be the best course of action for Pew upstream, though.

Yes, this is [known and documented](https://github.com/berdario/pew#workon-opens-a-new-shell-prompt)

Unfortunately, there's no cross platform way to fix this. Automatically modifying the user's configuration file could be a recipe for disaster.

Detecting if the user's prompt doesn't have such information and warn him about it could be an improvement, and a good enough workaround for this issue. I attempted [something like that](https://github.com/berdario/pew/commit/0adc21ca55118dea9bc334ee5ec7f131abed1f7b), but unfortunately that's impossible due to the fact that `PS1` is not a proper env variable, but it's "magic internal state" (or at least it seems to behave like this):

    bash -lc "echo \$PS1"

doesn't echo anything, nor does

    echo "echo \$PS1" | bash -l

work

I even tried by supplying a fake tty (maybe the problem is that `bash` doesn't create the `PS1` unless it's a tty)

```python
from subprocess import Popen, PIPE
import pty
import os
import sys
import tty

master, slave = pty.openpty()
p = Popen(['bash', '-l'], stdin=slave, stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE)
pin = os.fdopen(master, 'w')
tty.setcbreak(sys.stdin)

pin.write('echo $SHELL\n')
pin.write('echo $PS1\n')
pin.write('exit\n')
print(p.stdout.readline())
print(p.stdout.readline())
print(p.stdout.readline())
```

I've been doing a lot of thinking about this feature and simply don't see how I can make it work as formulated.   I believe it is inherently ill-defined as a context-manager.   Specifically, if I see code like this:
   
    async def spam():
            async with defer_cancellation:
                    await coro()
                    print('Done')

I fully expect that the "Done" message will appear no matter what happens with respect to cancellation.  I put it in the same category as code that might appear in a `finally:` block.  That code is supposed to run without cancellation.  A strong guarantee.

There are all sorts of problems with it though:

1.  If `coro()` uses `allow_cancellation`, it's broken.  Task can be cancelled.
2. If `coro()` itself uses `defer_cancellation`, it's broken. Task can be cancelled on exit from the inner deferred block.
3. If `coro()` gets a cancellation request internally and handles it, where does the task go from there?   It can't ignore the request.   It can't allow the task to keep running.  `coro()` has to report a failure somehow.  How does it do this exactly?  How does the cancellation proceed?  It's broken.
4. If `coro()` relies upon timeouts, it's  broken.   Timeouts can't be delivered if a `defer_cancellation` is in effect.

I just don't see any way out of this entanglement.  I think you could fix it if you reformulated `defer_cancellation` so that it launched code to be shielded from cancellation into its own task.  Maybe something like this:

    async def defer_cancellation(coro):
           task = await spawn(coro)
           try:
                 return await task.join()
           except CancelledError:
                 await task.join()      # Wait for operation to finish
                 raise

This is a lot simpler.  It also allows the shielded coroutine to use timeouts and other features.  Yes, it requires you to put the uninterruptible code into its own coroutine--so you'd lose the context-manager aspect of it.   However, I think the restored sanity makes up for it.

So. After reading over the python docs. Random is not guaranteed between different versions of python. Only guarantee is that a seed with produce the same results. Now with that said, it might be possible to gaurantee a result no matter the os or version. If we write a random function ourselves, it would be the same everywhere. I would suggest if going down that route, it would simply be a way to override the builtin random function in extreme cases like this. One way to do this is to generate a hash and convert that to a float. This should be good enough even with collisions here and there.

The tests are currently run, when environment variable `WITH_TIMINGS` is set to `1`. This is because I created an averaging system to reduce the variation caused by random things, with all the tests currently running 100 times. It runs each test, even if it's not critical, so we can e.g. asses the affect of optimizing the code.

The implementation also includes option for setting a limit for the average duration; a failure to meet the criteria causes the test to fail. This way we can easily check if we meet the requirements set by the specification, running on different hardwares. Because of this, the tests run with timings on are allowed failures in Travis CI, as the performance there cannot be predicted.

Hey @tkamaal 

Right now `play` is (somewhat opaquely) wrapped inside the `preview()` method of the `Transformer` and `Combiner` classes.

A minimal example would be:
```
import sox
tfm = sox.Transformer()
tfm.preview("path_to_a_file.wav")
```

any number of effects can be applied and previewed as well:
```
import sox
tfm = sox.Transformer()
tfm.reverb()
tfm.reverse()
tfm.preview("path_to_a_file.wav")
```

If these examples doesn't work on your system, it's likely because the sox installation on Windows doesn't come with the `play` binary. If so, please let me know!

I can't say we have - I've not heard of anyone running into rate-limit problems.  We're using XMPP to send the messages though, not the API, so the limit is different. I've been using two instances of will (one work, one personal) for a year and a half, without trouble.  The limit is ~150 messages / minute (this will go up to ~250 when some work to use the new tokens is done.)  It's conceivable that one could hit that, but it's a *lot* of talking for a bot to do.  

The HTML stuff is a bit of a pain, but most of us have worked around it by doing a plain-text message with mentions right after the HTML one.   I'm sure you read through that err issue, but to be clear, HTML messages work fine, they're just not *also* auto-highlighted (with mentions, github and image parsing, etc.)  Personally, I look at that as a feature, not a bug - when I've got content that I took the time to format HTML for, I generally don't want hipchat auto-converting it.  Mentions are the lone caveat, but for every purpose I've run across, a quick normal-message mention does trick well.

Let me know if you've got any other questions!

```python
@cached_property
def xs(self):
    """Return a tuple of this operator's arguments."""
    return tuple(_expr(node) for node in self.node.data())
```

Since it uses `cached_property`, it will store that value permanently.

I'm just paranoid about users doing stupid things.  Having it separate gives them an opportunity to do something like recompile with different options and then run a `make install` which overwrites the config.h, and now the headers are wrong for one of the compiled libs.  

This isn't hypothetical either.  There are a huge number of dlib users who have no idea what a shared library even is.  For instance, the simplest way to use dlib is to simply call `add_subdirectory` rather than do any kind of install.  But I very regularly get people asking questions like "I built a .so, no what?".  It's also very common for people to build part of dlib in one configuration and then mix in object files built with a different configuration, creating a bunch of ODR violations.  Recently, I've honestly considered making CMake emit an error when users attempt to build dlib in any other way aside from calling `add_subdirectory`  (e.g. like this https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/examples/CMakeLists.txt).  For instance, it could emit a big message saying that using `add_subdirectory` is better and only people who really know what they are doing or are package maintainers should build it standalone.  

I also have problems where package maintainers will compile dlib in a funny way, like without GUI support for instance.  Users will then complain to **_me_** about it.  So I've considered making it an error to compile dlib standalone without a GUI.

All of the above seems insanely heavy handed so I haven't done it.  But I get many emails every day about this kind of stuff, it's only so long until I lose my cool about it :(.  How irritating do you think it would be if it was required to say this to compile a standalone dlib library `cmake -DI_KNOW_WHAT_IM_DOING=1 dlib`? :)

Anyway, I think the thing you are proposing is probably fine.  I'm just worried I'm going to find out there are a bunch of people who habitually set BUILD_SHARED_LIBS to ON and will be surprised by this when they get runtime linker errors because the loader can't find their dlib shared library.  Maybe that's not an issue.  I'm sure I'll find out though if it is.

Here is another kind of example of the stuff I have to worry about: https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/dlib/dlib_basic_cpp_build_tutorial.txt.  I used to be inundated with users bitching about how one thing or another doesn't work in dlib only to find out they were `#including` some of dlib's source files, and obviously getting linker errors.  I have a bunch of preprocessor magic now that detects that and makes their compiler output that message in dlib_basic_cpp_build_tutorial.  This problem is made even worse since there are blog posts on the internet telling people to `#include` .cpp files in dlib. But now that this error message is there it has significantly cut down on the number of emails I get about this.

It wasn't always so bad, but now that machine learning is super popular I get a very large number of users who have no idea what they are doing, many have never programmed before.  So I try really hard to make things as simple and foolproof as possible.  For instance, for many dlib users, simply installing boost is an insurmountable challenge.  I know this because boost is required to use dlib's python API and I regularly get complaints like "I've been trying to install boost for 10 hours and I finally gave up!".  

Anyway, sorry for the rant.  I think your proposal is fine.

> If I'm using OID why do I need an MIB?

That's because you are passing a value (2) along with the OID. The value is untyped (to SNMP). To send a well formed packet, pysnmp needs [to figure](https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/blob/master/pysnmp/smi/rfc1902.py#L852) out SNMP type of the value you are sending. The only way for pysnmp to handle this is to look up the MIB. But pysnmp can't [yet] figure what MIB it should load to get hold of MIB object identified by that OID (e.g. OID->MIB mapping is not implemented).

To fix that you simply need to give it SNMP type for your value:

    ObjectType(ObjectIdentity('1.3.6.1.4.1.9.9.96.1.1.1.1.2.100'), Integer32(2))

> My understanding of this is zc.buildout is able to pretend to be a sphinx application?!!!

Not really, ``buildout`` is 'just' a far superior alternative to virtual environments. Basically all the packages can be installed as 'eggs' in one directory (you may want to create a ``~/.buildout/default.cfg`` file containing [this](https://pastebin.com/1ctZeng8)), and every project picks the eggs it needs. No need to have one (or more!) big fat virtual env for every project and install the same dependencies X times. If you look at the created scripts (bin/tests on Unix, bin/tests-script.py on Windows) you'll understand what's going on: the scripts simply add paths of available eggs to ``sys.path`` and then runs whatever script you tell it to run. There are hundreds of buildout recipes available to generate all sorts of scripts. I just can't do without it anymore, it's so convenient!

Great!  Ok, let me try to answer your questions:

1.  If you add a key to Redis, by default, it will never be deleted until you delete it manually.  That is, unless you specify an expiration time.  You can set an expiration time like this:

    `>>> redis.expire('key', 10)`

    This command means that Redis will expire your key in 10 seconds (as the expiration time is specified in seconds).

2.  The easiest way to delete your entire Redis database is:

    `>>> redis.flushall()`

3.  You can make temporary keys with Pottery.  Just instantiate your `RedisSet`, `RedisDict`, or `RedisList` without specifying a key like so:

    `>>> tmp = RedisDict(redis=redis)`

    Pottery will assign its own temporary key, and Pottery will also delete your temporary `RedisDict` as soon as it goes out of scope.

Please let me know if that helps!  Good luck!

Thanks for the efforts you've put in!  The API can be a bit hairy; it developed over time, as I discovered more nooks and crannies in EtherNet/IP CIP...

When you use the poll API, if you want to use Tags, make sure you set the 'pass_thru=True' keyword.  Otherwise, every parameter processed by the 'parameter_substitution' method is expected to be set up in the parmaters (or self.PARAMETERS) dictionaries.  With this set, anything not found will be assumed to be a Tag name, and will be passed thru.

Give that a try, and see if there is anything else.


Also, when you get a None back -- this generally the flag that means that there was an error in reading the target attribute.  Unfortunately, you'll need to increase logging to get more details about the failure.  If you are using some of the advanced cpppo.server.enip.client APIs that return a large tuple of details about the operation (eg. client.connector.pipeline), then one of the tuple elements will be the CIP status code.  However, increasing logging has usually been most useful to me.

And while the filter works, it diverges from the data. But if I change N to 10000, it eventually converges again. 

dt is very small, so N=100 is a tiny bit of time: 1/00 sec. 

I changed the initialization of x to np.array([0., 10000., 0.]). Which is really just [0, v/dt, 0] where v/epoch=1.0.

Now the filter tracks fine and doesn't crash. 

Another way is to realize that you are really over certain in your definition of P. You have P=diag([3,3,3]), yet the variance is really far greater - you are giving an initial velocity of 0, but  the actual velocity is 10000.

Of course, this is my data, not yours, you may be encountering a different problem, but one way or another I suspect your filter is not well designed for your data.

I'm not entirely sure how the IMM code is interacting with this. Presumably there is a divide by zero somewhere. I can look at that later. This is actually a really good illustration of the difference between pedagogical code and production code. If you just code the equations from the books you get something that is theoretically correct yet works poorly in practice Or, I just have a bug in my IMM code ;). 

Basically, though, you have to be careful with initializing x; I should stress that more. It may not 'matter' when running a single KF, except the output is poor, but once you start trying to use that information in other ways all heck can break loose because you do not have a well performing filter. In practice I write code that initialize v and a after the 2 and 3rd update if running real time, and pre-compute it if running in batch mode. I.e. v_0 = z[1] - z[0] if z is in state space.

@craffel just one quick question: to compute the overlap ratio you need to compute note matching, and then the metric is derived from the matched notes similar to precision, recall and f1. Computationally the most efficient thing to do would be to add this metric to the existing `precision_recall_f1` function since it already computes the note matching. The alternative would be to implement the metric in a new function which also computes the note matching, but this means exactly the same note matching would be computed twice when the evaluator is called (and this where most of the computational load is).

So... add the metric to `precision_recall_f1` (and if so, rename function?), or implement in separate function?

Where I know it exists for sure is ARM. ARM has the conecpt of big/little systems, where that doesn't refer to byte order but cpu capabilities. You have for example 2 high power cores (big) with many functional units, out-of-order and superscalar processing and such. Next to that you have 4 low power cores (little) with few functional units and way less speed. Note: the cores have the same opcodes so code runs on either, just faster on big cores at a big power drain.

I'm not sure how other event-loop based servers solve this problem, maybe by routinely invoking the Python interpreter.

Heh, so this is interesting. The biggest issue here is that a comment is actually a syntactic element in XML. There's no real syntax for INI files, but most INI parsers also recognize INI comments.
For JSON and Yaml, this would be much more complicated. In fact, the Yaml specification forbids parsing comments altogether [1].

It should be possible to provide comments serialization in XML and INI and ignore these in Yaml and JSON. I'd have to rewrite the INI parsing logic first to return `OrderedDict`, but that shouldn't be a big deal; XML parsing already returns that. Then I'd have to decide on a special key in the returned structures that would represent comments, something like `#comment-follows-this-is-very-unique-so-we-can-be-sure-noone-actually-uses-that-as-key`. Or similar :)

I think this should also be an argument for API functions to explicitly say that I want/don't want to parse/serialize comments.

Would that that sufficient for you?

[1] http://yaml.org/spec/current.html#comment/information%20model

The way I've implemented it is that setting `link_by_default` to `true` applies to **import** only (allows to `import` a dotfile and automatically set the `link` key to `true` without having to use `-l --link` on the command line).  From your answer I guess you wanted to change the default behavior for **install** (thus having dotfiles without a `link` key being considered `link: true` instead of the current default `link: false`). 

Dotfiles already imported without a `link` key will per default have `link: false`. I don't think changing this is a good idea for multiple reasons
* dotfiles are either initially imported as links (using `-l --link` or by having `link_by_default: true`)
* or are manually added/modified in the config file and their `link` key can be set during the same edition session

And finally because it could potentially completely mess with people's dotfiles if they mistakenly change that value.

I have however coded a small python script ([change-link.py](https://github.com/deadc0de6/dotdrop/blob/link_by_default/scripts/change-link.py)) available in the `scripts` directory that allows to mass update all dotfiles `link` key.

For example:
```bash
# set all "link" keys to true
# except for the dotfiles f_vimrc and f_xinitrc
./change-link.py --true ../config.yaml --ignore f_vimrc --ignore f_xinitrc
```

This should allow you to mass update your config file to make it look the way you want hopefully. If you need more options in that script, feel free to ask me or do a PR.
Hope this helps.

Well, no.

> With a couple of lines of config WhiteNoise allows your web app to serve its own static files

Well, many (most?) frameworks come with support for serving static files already - https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/howto/static-files/

WhiteNoise itself can't make the decision to use or not use `sendfile` because it doesn't have direct access to the socket. It calls `wsgi.file_wrapper` and it's up to the WSGI server whether it implements that using `sendfile` or not. Gunicorn does, but not all other WSGI servers do.

Of course nginx uses `sendfile` as well, so when comparing the two you're really comparing how fast they can parse HTTP headers, and nginx easily wins.

I'll add a note to produce some benchmarks when I have the time.

I don't know how I feel about about "downsampling" the colors though. I can picture someone coming up with a really clean 256 color scheme that just gets butchered when you try to convert it to 8 colors. `~/.i3/py3status` > `~/.config/i3status/py3status/` > `~/.config/i3/py3status/`

I removed the sorted as we should look in the directories in a user set order if provided by `-i` as that is what the user would expect.  Also this means that we look in our predefined directories in a set order.

If the foreign device is allowed to communicate without being registered, then the only packets that should dropped are `ForwardedNPDU` because the message could have been intended for a foreign device application instance using the same socket.  In all cases `OriginalBroadcastNPDU` should be silently dropped because maybe the application happened to pick a random socket that corresponds to a network.

**Proposal:**
- Intermediate step: objects get their own field of configurable items, which the superconfigurator uses. 
- Object *may* supply callback for configuration
- Standard library of configurations for basic test options (which cmdline can use)
- Configuration handler can read from a set of global options and pass along a chain of responsibility for config items (each tries to handle the config, if missing, it fails)
- Use of closures/properties to get values where templating is used. --> May be better than use of simple dictionary scopes, b/c of encapsulation?
- Configurable objects have a field defining their configurable attributes.
- Scopes become a set of getarrs on the scope objects (or use of a resolve(context) method, similar to Context object fetching using variables for templating)

I respect your POV but I disagree with it. Here's why:

- this separate file is like configuration file. Zero-conf tools are more attractive and easier to both execute and start with
- easier refactor: It's easier to change single place than two (e.g. function name). With Celery it's even more important, because function name is usually also a task name
- coupling: it's better when things are nearby each other

I agree that comments reduce readability, but there's no other known mechanism that is coupled with function/statement, doesn't influence generated code and could be used by 3rd-party tool like vulture.

Getting back to the topic of this issue: vulture should use `whitelists/stdlib.py` by default. For now, I don't think we need an option to disable this feature. @RJ722 Feel free to open a pull request for this.

Hm, interesting! That's certainly a great use-case.

The PAKE algorithm doesn't really support multiple connections, though. The security comes from it being a single-use code, so if the sender is willing to re-use the same code for 200 connections, we'd be giving an attacker 200x the chance to guess it. I could imagine a mode where it'd accept multiple connections as long as each of them succeeded (i.e. make connections one at a time, when one succeeds, allow the next one to start, but terminate immediately upon the first failure and don't accept any subsequent attempts). I think that'd mitigate the attack-multiplier, but the usability would suffer. I'd feel pretty bad being the audience member who fumbled the code and then forced the presenter to restart the process and announce a new code.

If we weren't trying to make it secure, we could use the code (in fact just the channel-id) as a broadcast-channel identifier, and everybody would just get whatever they get. It's probably ok to give up confidentiality for a broadcast like this, but it'd be nice if an attacker couldn't break integrity (modifying the tutorial en-route).

Let's see, what protocols/tools could we use to maintain integrity? The only thing I can think of is:

* publish the channel-ID and a hash of the file, everybody downloads the file and then manually compares the hash. Maybe after everyone has downloaded it, the presenter reads out the hash, and everybody compares it against what's on their screen

Any other ideas?

For the actual transmission of the bits, Bittorrent would be a great distribution mechanism, and whatever we come up with for the PAKE/wormhole side could just be responsible for distributing the .torrent metadata file. But I totally agree that actually integrating a BT client into this tool would be a big hassle. Is there any pure-python Bittorrent implementation out there?

Hi @marcbllv,

I have viewed your codes. So great to also have the interpreter for Gradient Boost Trees! However, I found some potential issues.  
According to sklearn's user guide here http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/ensemble.html#gradient-boosting,  GBRT builds the additive model in a forward stagewise fashion:  
  
![](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/_images/math/7b0b481e5f2c318df259c4a1021745d07f19b8d8.png)
  
![](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/_images/math/eac4fdf64ca91da58bfcbda90a05067118d52429.png)  
  
Where the step length is chosen using line search:  
![](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/_images/math/fe9f5a6b17d269a906e91f358e1118bb4838dcf0.png)
  
Thus, to interpret GBM, the step value could't be ignored. I have also checked the source codes:
In the `_fit_stage` function, codes followed update value of each tree leaf.
```python
loss.update_terminal_regions(tree.tree_, X, y, residual, y_pred,
                                         sample_weight, sample_mask,
                                         self.learning_rate, k=k)
```
Details about the implementation of function `update_terminal_regions` depends on which kind of loss objective is used. I have deep dive into `LeastSquaresError`(for Gradient Boosting Regressor) and `BinomialDeviance`(for Gradient Boosting Binary Classifier).  
  
For `LeastSquaresError`, which is always used for regression problem, things go quite simple. As mentioned as followed, terminal regions need not to be updated for least squares. For GradientBoostingRegressor, feature contributions for each tree branch can be calculated the same way as RandomForest, because values of tree nodes from the same branch are consistent.
```python
class LeastSquaresError(RegressionLossFunction):
    """Loss function for least squares (LS) estimation.
    Terminal regions need not to be updated for least squares. """
    def init_estimator(self):
        return MeanEstimator()

    def __call__(self, y, pred, sample_weight=None):
        if sample_weight is None:
            return np.mean((y - pred.ravel()) ** 2.0)
        else:
            return (1.0 / sample_weight.sum() *
                    np.sum(sample_weight * ((y - pred.ravel()) ** 2.0)))

    def negative_gradient(self, y, pred, **kargs):
        return y - pred.ravel()

    def update_terminal_regions(self, tree, X, y, residual, y_pred,
                                sample_weight, sample_mask,
                                learning_rate=1.0, k=0):
        """Least squares does not need to update terminal regions.
        But it has to update the predictions.
        """
        # update predictions
        y_pred[:, k] += learning_rate * tree.predict(X).ravel()

    def _update_terminal_region(self, tree, terminal_regions, leaf, X, y,
                                residual, pred, sample_weight):
        pass
```

However, for  `BinomialDeviance`, which is always used for GradientBoostingClassifier, things are much more complicated. As showed in the codes followed, value of each terminal tree node (leaf) is updated. Thus, values of tree nodes from the same tree branch are not consistent. When calculating the feature contributions for each tree, the step value couldn't be ignored. Values of parent nodes should be modified in the same way as leaf node to make sure that all node values are consistent. Then the same logic can be applied on the modified trees.
```python
def _update_terminal_region(self, tree, terminal_regions, leaf, X, y, residual, pred, sample_weight):
    """Make a single Newton-Raphson step.
    our node estimate is given by:
        sum(w * (y - prob)) / sum(w * prob * (1 - prob))
    we take advantage that: y - prob = residual
    """
    terminal_region = np.where(terminal_regions == leaf)[0]
    residual = residual.take(terminal_region, axis=0)
    y = y.take(terminal_region, axis=0)
    sample_weight = sample_weight.take(terminal_region, axis=0)

    numerator = np.sum(sample_weight * residual)
    denominator = np.sum(sample_weight * (y - residual) * (1 - y + residual))

    if denominator == 0.0:
        tree.value[leaf, 0, 0] = 0.0
    else:
        tree.value[leaf, 0, 0] = numerator / denominator
```  

To prove this, I modified the codes of the GradientBoosting module to store the original trees (whose tree leaves are not updated) and compared them with the modified trees extracted from bst.estomators_. It turned out that only the leaf values of the original trees were updated. Values of parent nodes are all the same.

This warning message occurs because a half-closed connection, which is not supported SSL, see [Protocol.eof_received()](https://docs.python.org/dev/library/asyncio-protocol.html#asyncio.Protocol.eof_received).

It occurs after we establish a secure connection to the proxy judge, and when the page is requested. For some reason some proxies at this point, terminate the connection by sending EOF (when you enabled logging, you can see that often it is the same proxy).

In code:

```python
# proxy.py#L193
resp = await asyncio.wait_for(

```

If we do check for EOF prior to this string of (`self._reader.at_eof()`), we see that EOF is False. But after this string EOF is True and the warning is already added. So I don't see any possibility to intercept the warning about receiving EOF when SSL is active =(

@ericvsmith @gvanrossum 

I still think that we need ``dict_factory`` argument. This is not only about ``dict`` vs ``OrderedDict``, there are also popular third party containers, for example ``SortedDict`` that provide even stronger guarantees than ``OrderedDict``. The same applies to ``tuple_factory`` that can be used for conversion/compatibility to legacy namedtuple APIs.

Also I think we need ``copy_function``, this not only about ``copy`` vs ``deepcopy`` and not only about recursion. Many classes have their custom copy functions, as I already mentioned above. Another problem is that if we remove this argument, the default will be not satisfiable for many (see long thread about mutable default values and whether to copy them).

The logic behind these two arguments is simple: without these two arguments we can satisfy 50% of users, with them we can satisfy 90% of users, and the point is not about 2x more satisfied users, it is about 5x less unsatisfied users. For example two bullets by @taleinat will be fixed by these arguments.

I agree that we need to look inside built-in containers for recursive copy and with other changes proposed so far. To summarize:
* Switch to ``nested=True`` to be recursive by default
* Add code to also look inside built-in containers
* Move ``copy_function`` to the end of signature
* Switch to ``copy_function=None`` by default

For the first part, I think you are facing issues described in this paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/physics/0607100

Basically, the best partition in term of modularity can be an aggregation or a division from a big cluster. For instance if you have several connected cliques, the partition maximizing the modularity may merge some cliques even if it has no true meaning.


For the second part, it is complicated. Reference is   "Laplacian Dynamics and Multiscale Modular Structure in Networks", Lambiotte, J.-C. Delvenne, M. Barahona

A way to explain it is through random walks. A community can be seen as a trap for a random walker on the graph if it moves for a given distance. If you increase this distance, the walker may go further, and thus traps/communities are bigger. If you decrease it, the walker may go closer, and communities are smaller. I do not know the relationship between this "distance" and the resolution parameter. You should read their articles and the following ones if you need more details.

Ultimately this error is from pytest complaining that it tried to import a conftest file but ended up importing from the virtualenv path instead. The `tests` directory/module isn't actually meant to be copied into the virtualenv path because it's ignored in `setup.py`. Potentially your version of `setuptools` is old and is not obeying `packages=find_packages(exclude=['tests', 'tests.*']),
`, but I doubt that. I assume you didn't add another package to one of the requirements files that happens to install a `tests.conftest` module. Try without your edits to the settings file. There's an outside chance that the file doesn't import properly and it's breaking the mechanism in pytest.

Otherwise, check the pytest issues for this: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=ImportMismatchError%20 . I see they have one where they are discussing actually removing this check because it's easy to trigger under `tox`.

@HariSekhon If your program that is using GPL code is published it must be pusblished under GPL license in it's entirity. However if you are using a license that is **compatible** with GPL you can mix and match them.

> Many of the most common free software licenses, especially the permissive licenses, such as the original MIT/X license, BSD licenses (in the three-clause and two-clause forms, though not the original four-clause form), MPL 2.0, and LGPL, are GPL-compatible. That is, their code can be combined with a program under the GPL without conflict and the new combination would have the GPL applied to the whole (not the other license).
Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/License_compatibility#GPL_compatibility

So AFAIK you can use GPL2 `dicttoxml` package in your modified BSD program, the whole program would need to be licensed under GPL but the pieces that are not `dicttoxml` in other words - your pieces, can be licensed under your BSD license.

I appreciate the effort, but I think I'm going to have to decline. The nice part of a Javascript interpreter is that each time Cloudflare changes their code, it's pretty trivial to account for the new changes. With a regex, you may have to significantly modify your code, and if they're clever, they may someday make the challenge scripts dynamic enough that it's essentially impossible to parse properly without emulating the Javascript.

The security risks are nearly non-existent at the moment, because I restrict the Javascript engines to either plain V8, or sandboxed Node. Barring a critical exploit in either V8 or Node (which would greatly affect hundreds of thousands of other projects), the worst someone can do is a `while(true){}` and keep your process hanging forever. Someone could probably achieve the same effect with many regular expressions, by crafting a pathological string which the regex repeatedly backtracks on.

Admittedly, Cloudflare only seems to change their code about once a year on average, but that may change at any time, and I don't see the value in switching to a more crude form of parsing.

I still didn't got a round to investigate this in detail.  But after having a short glance on the pytest-xdist documentation, I assume the problem is the following: pytest-xdist features to run the tests in parallel.  It seem to do this in parallel processes, each running its own pytest instance.  As a result, the dependent test is skipped because its dependency has not been run (in the same parallel instance of pytest).  I'm afraid, this is fundamentally incompatible with pytest-dependency, because such a parallelization can only work if the tests are independent from each other.  But the very purpose of pytest-dependency is to handle the case that they are not.

If this assumption turn out to be true, the only thing pytest-dependency can do about this problem, is to disable the parallelization in pytest-xdist.  I would still need to figure out how to do this.

    def tearDown(self):

        django.db.models.signals.pre_delete.disconnect(self.mock_pre_delete, sender=self.model)

        instance = self.model.objects.get(id=987)
        with reversion.create_revision():
            instance.delete()
        self.assertEqual(
            list(Version.objects.get_for_model(self.model).values_list("object_id", "serialized_data")), [
                (
                    "987",
                    '[{"model": "test_app.testmodel", "pk": 987, "fields": {"name": "created", "related": []}}]'
                ),
            ])

The fastest way is using a Cypher query
```sql
match (node { id_prop: XX })-[r:RELATIONSHIP_TYPE]->() delete r;
```

Using the `neo4jrestclient` API you would need to iterate over all the relationships and delete them one by one. It can be done in a transaction, but I would still recommend using a Cypher query.
```python
for rel in node.relationships.outgoing(types=["RELATIONSHIP_TYPE"]):
    rel.delete()
```

So the idea is to allow this kind of code

```
def foo(x):
    if hasattr(__module__, 'pythran'):
        # pythran implem
    else:
        # python implem
```

This would behave somehow like a preprocessing step, ``#ifdef PYTHRAN``. Unfortunaltely, this is very ad-hoc (what about ``if not not hasattr(__module__, 'pythran'):`` ? there are multiple ways of expressiing the condition, store the expression in a global etc).

I don't want to make it an idiom, nor do I want to implement a generic pre procesisng step. Note that because any Python code could go in the else section... So it's not a valid Pythran inout and the preprocessing step should be ware of that.

There's still the currently working solution to put the pythonic version of foo in the calling module and test wether the imported pythran module has the '__pythran__' attribute, isn't that enough?

transparent for the Python code that performs Unicode I/O and
`win-unicode-console` would be shipped with Python. It is too
intrusive otherwise to force users to modify their global environment
in a way that might break other Python scripts. `win-unicode-console`
should provide a solution even for those people who won't modify
`sitecustomize`, `usercustomize`, and the like. Usually, I recommend
`py -mrun` approach but it is nice to have an option to enable it from
within a script too.

> ..But you can still do it in a multiplatform way – just make it
> conditional, testing the platform.

Yes, I can do it as well as all other developers that use your module
and need a cross-platform support.  Or we can do it in a
single place:

    try:
        from win_unicode_console import streams, console, readline_hook
    except (AttributeError, ImportError):
        enable = lambda **kw: None
        disable = lambda: None
    else: # the rest of the win-console-unicode/__init__.py

that is all: `win-unicode-console` should provide useful behavior on
Unix, namely: it should do nothing there -- in particular, it should
avoid `ImportError`.

Any additional behavior would be an _enhancement_. `win-unicode-console`
mission: *"enable Unicode input and display when running Python from
Windows console"* could be _extended_: *"enable Unicode input and
display when running Python."*

Currently, Python unreasonably respects misconfigured ASCII locale
settings. Usually, `PYTHONIOENCODING` could be used to force utf-8.
As an enhancement, `win-unicode-console` may provide a similar (but
perhaps more aggresive and less general) option from within a
script. See [related discussion][1].

Example:

    #!/usr/bin/env python
    import codecs
    import sys
    import win_unicode_console # $ pip install win_unicode_console

    broken_environment = (sys.getfilesystemencoding() == 'ascii' and
        codecs.lookup(sys.stdout.encoding) == codecs.lookup('ascii'))
    win_unicode_console.enable(use_utf8=broken_environment)
    print(u"\N{SNOWMAN}")

The only difference compared to the current implementation is that it
enables utf-8 streams on top of binary API instead of utf-16le streams
on top of Unicode API. Use-case: enable Unicode input and display in
Python REPL started via ssh on a remote Linux box.

The preferred solution would be to fix the environment but we live in
an imperfect world where it is not always possible.

`use_utf8` is an enhancement. Just "do nothing on Unix" option would
be fine.

[1]: https://bugs.python.org/issue19846#msg205564

@tomchristie - To give some background. What I was trying to achieve was a documentation of `profile` parameter on my endpoint, which is an embedded document with it's own schema. `SchemaGenerator` specifies the type as `'object'`, where it should be a type on it's own, like `'UserProfile'`.

When using `OpenAPI`, you can document such field by setting custom type. In `OpenAPI` this type is defined by `definitions` section. This is something you can not do with `CoreAPI` as far as I know, even tough the `CoreAPI` `Field.type` takes arbitrary string and you could put `'UserProfile'` there.

The spec mismatch makes it hard to properly document endpoint parameters, utilizing `OpenAPI` type definitions. This is something I require, otherwise the generated OpenAPI spec is worthless for me.

Hi there,

Sorry for the (very) late response. There are a couple of ways to do what you want:

* You can override the [on_completion()](https://github.com/juliomalegria/django-chunked-upload/blob/master/chunked_upload/views.py#L232) method of ChunkedUploadCompleteView [1] to delete the instance once you've used the uploaded file (saved it somewhere else, processed it, etc.). When deleting the instance, don't forget to pass delete_file=True (see [BaseChunkUpload.delete](https://github.com/juliomalegria/django-chunked-upload/blob/master/chunked_upload/models.py#L61)).

* The problem with the previous solution is that the incomplete or failing ChunkedUploads will pile up (since they never get to run on_completion). So the other option is to run the management command [delete_expired_upload](https://github.com/juliomalegria/django-chunked-upload/blob/master/chunked_upload/management/commands/delete_expired_uploads.py). This commands cleans the inactive chunks based on [CHUNKED_UPLOAD_EXPIRATION_DELTA](https://github.com/juliomalegria/django-chunked-upload#chunked_upload_expiration_delta). You can use a cron job to run this once a day/week/etc. Read [StackOverflow: Django custom command and cron](http://stackoverflow.com/q/1601153/972208) for how to do this.

Awesome, thanks for the quick response.

It doesn't shutdown the server process if there active connections to the redis server process that where not created by the current process.  

If all activity to the redis server is through redislite Redis or StrictRedis classes then the last process with a connection to the redis server will terminate the redis server.  If however a connection is made without going through redislite it's possible for the redis-server process to remain running if that connection is still active when the process that has redislite running terminates.

Ok, I understand why it happens:
reset() function in Python calls BeforeFirst function in C interface, which is supposed to reset the iterator. BeforeFirst function is also used after the epoch ends to reset the iterator. Since the dataset size may be indivisible by the batch size (and it is in the example shown by @chenboheng), by default ImageRecordIter resets itself and takes images from the next epoch to fill the last batch (it is shown in the first output shown in this issue - 2 last images from the last batch are the same as 2 first images of the first batch). Then the "reset" of iterator does not actually reset it - if it was, it would read those 2 images twice. 
In order to improve performance, ImageRecordIter prefetches a few batches of data. It turns out that this dataset is small enough that the iterator prefetches all of it in those 2 seconds when the main thread sleeps, and so it reaches the end of epoch internally. Calling reset in that situation calls BeforeFirst in the same way it would be called after epoch is done, so it does not actually reset the iterator, but prepares it for a new epoch, with dataset being offset by those 2 images that it used to fill the last batch of the "previous epoch". That is why the result is different.  
So basically the bug can only be observed if the dataset is small enough that it fits in internal prefetch queue, the size of dataset is not divisible by the batch size and we give it enough time to internally process the entire dataset.

I guess the possible solution would be to treat the BeforeFirst as the end of epoch and not hard reset only if the end of epoch was actually reached through calls to next. I will look into fixing this. 
Still, the "reset" name kind of suggests it will completely reset the iterator and that is not the case if you reach end of epoch. @piiswrong What do you think about it? Is it acceptable?

As we notice, the variable `dropout` is used twice in this code snippet, once in `FusedRNNCell` and the other time in `outputs = mx.sym.Dropout(outputs, p=dropout)`. What I mean is that **the first use has zero effect** because `CuDNN-RNN` does not apply dropout when the RNN has only 1 layer.

For anyone interested in picking this up, here's the suggested plan of attack:

1. Look at `watchman_ops` in `watchman.h` to get a feel for the interface you'll need to implement.  You'll need to create `watcher/poll.c` and hook it up in `Makefile.am`.  There are several "methods" that can be implemented for a watcher.  For the polling watcher, you can make the the `(start|stop)_watch_(file|dir)` methods NOPs because you're going to be recursively watching the root and don't need any special handling on a per-file or per-dir basis.
2. The polling watcher should spawn a thread that performs the polling.  There are some thread synchronization concerns here; you can draw on the implementation of `watcher/fsevents.c` for managing signalling and starting and stopping threads.
3. The polling thread is conceptually very simple: in a loop, sleep for some configurable poll interval, then recursively walk the directory tree.  For each filename that has an mtime newer than the last loop crawl time, add the filename to a pending list (use `w_pending_coll_add` for that).  After crawling, if the pending list you built is not empty, use `pthread_cond_signal` to wake up the main watchman IO thread.  Look at the `fse_callback` function for inspiration.
4. Your implementations of `wait_notify` and `consume` notify can be similar to those functions in `fsevents.c`, but use the pending list you built instead of an ad-hoc list of entries that `fsevents.c` uses; the `consume_notify` method will use `w_pending_coll_append` to transfer the contents of your pending list into the one that the main IO thread will consume.

Hi @eagle-r - I think this is due to the use of prefixes in your `STATICFILES_DIRS` setting. Currently, django-libsass just passes a plain list of include paths to the libsass compiler, so while we can add node_modules/bootstrap-sass/assets/stylesheets/bootstrap to the search path, we can't tell it "when you see the path css/bootstrap, map that to node_modules/bootstrap-sass/assets/stylesheets/bootstrap instead". The easiest workaround is probably to write

    STATICFILES_DIRS = [
        _Path(BASE_DIR, 'node_modules/bootstrap-sass/assets/stylesheets'),
        _Path(BASE_DIR, 'django-site/django_app/static'),
    ]

and use `@import "bootstrap/variables"`.

However, I've just seen that libsass-python now supports [import callbacks](https://hongminhee.org/libsass-python/sass.html#id5) as of version 0.10, which would allow us to support these kinds of mappings. So that's something for the wish list...

I understand the issue you are talking about. I wrote the following mini-test to illustrate all the possible combinations.

What you are saying is that Test Case 1a should be FAIL. But in my opinion it's better to receive an Exception (feedback) than to have a silently running ProcessDevice de-attached to anything that keeps your ports reserved as in Test Case 2a. That is hard(er) to troubleshoot than just getting the nice exception.

But you do have a point with the fact that in 1a type of scenarios, one might need a controlled stop where the Proxy is the last one going down (and not right away) to allow for all messages to be sent around. I'll have a look at your idea to detach it from the process group.

Test Case | main | ProcessDevice | Results | Pass/Fail
---|---|---|---|---
1a CTRL+C pressed | SIG_IGN | SIG_DFL | ProcessDevice dies with KeyboardInterrupt; main keeps running until end | PASS
1b CTRL+C not pressed | SIG_IGN | SIG_DFL | ProcessDevice and main keep running until end | PASS
2a CTRL+C pressed | SIG_DFL | SIG_IGN | main quits abruptly but ProcessDevice keeps running in background | FAIL
2b CTRL+C not pressed | SIG_DFL | SIG_IGN | main never finishes as it hangs on join() | FAIL

```
import zmq
import zmq.devices
import time
import signal

# Test Cases
# 1 - main process: SIG_IGN, ProcessDevice: SIG_DFL
#       a - press CTRL+C while sleep()'ing
#       b - wait for script to finish
# 2 - main process: SIG_DFL, ProcessDevice: SIG_IGN
#       a - press CTRL+C while sleep()'ing
#       b - wait for script to finish
# 3 - main process: SIG_IGN, ProcessDevice: SIG_IGN (obvious)
# 4 - main process: SIG_DFL, ProcessDevice: SIG_DFL (obvious)
test_case = 2

p = zmq.devices.ProcessProxy(zmq.ROUTER, zmq.DEALER)
p.bind_in('tcp://127.0.0.1:5558')
p.bind_out('tcp://127.0.0.1:5556')
p.setsockopt_in(zmq.LINGER, 0)
p.setsockopt_out(zmq.LINGER, 0)

if test_case in [2, 3]:
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_IGN)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal.SIG_IGN)
p.start() # ProcessDevices inherits signal handler at creation time

# Main process ignores
if test_case in [1, 3]:
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_IGN)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal.SIG_IGN)
else:
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_DFL)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal.SIG_DFL)
time.sleep(60)

p.launcher.terminate()
p.join()
print('done')
```

URL validation is outside the scope of Bleach.

Bleach sanitizes HTML attribute values that contain URIs using this code:

https://github.com/mozilla/bleach/blob/b5ad8572b790df453fb844859c7b22d24bd974fd/bleach/sanitizer.py#L581

And linkifies url-ish things with this code:

https://github.com/mozilla/bleach/blob/b5ad8572b790df453fb844859c7b22d24bd974fd/bleach/linkifier.py#L60

If you want to use one of those, that's cool, but it's not a supported use of Bleach and liable to change at any time and it's likely to hedge towards "this substring of this larger string is probably a URL" and not towards "this string is a valid URL".

What is the value for `AUTH_REQUIRED` ? If it is `False` then API keys aren't necessary.

Generating a `500` is not great though. What would you expect the response should be? Should the API return `400 Bad Request` and explain that you can't create API keys if authentication is not enabled? Or should the API server allow API keys to be created even though they are ignored.

Make sure the API and the web console are **both** on HTTPS if you have decided to use SSL encryption. You're getting a "mixed content" error because some requests are being made to HTTP not HTTPS endpoints.

Alerta definitely works on HTTPS if configured properly. There is an example running on [Heroku](https://alerta.herokuapp.com/#/alerts?status=open) that uses HTTPS.

Note: `alerta.conf` is the config file for the `alerta` command-line tool and it is an INI-file format. `alertad.conf` is the config file for the `alertad` server and is just a plain python file.

To be precise, the issue is caused by Python 2 trying to auto-cast the raw credential string into unicode by an implicit `.decode('ascii')`, which fails when the raw string accidentally contains non-ascii content.

There are multiple Credential consumers in auth.py, and multiple Credential providers in credentials.py. And there may be more in future which may not pay attention to this detail. So it seems reasonable to have the microsurgery in the single critical point, the Credential class. I can add more safely logic though, to ensure we do not have extra `.decode("utf-8")` when the raw input happens to be unicode already, so that it would be future-proof. Will that helps?

After discussing with @bertjwregeer and @dstufft I'm worried about changing this in the original way described.

The core issue here is that there is no valid transaction during exception views, `NewResponse`, or `request.add_finished_callback` callbacks. Even if the transaction is aborted later, it is still a big problem that there isn't one to be used at all. It leads to the following subtle bugs:

1) `DetachedInstanceErrors` if touching request properties populated during the normal request such as `request.user`. This property was cached, and now the session was closed by pyramid_tm before the exception view executes. So the ref is alive but pointing to a detached object.

2) Database sessions left open. If you perform a database query in an exception view *right now* another transaction is automatically opened but it is never closed. This is bad.

I'm struggling to come up with a good way to fix this in a way that is sane.

I use the same sort of thing in my `pyramid_keystone` project to verify that the user has registered a `session` because without a `session` my project won't work:

https://github.com/bertjwregeer/pyramid_keystone/blob/master/pyramid_keystone/__init__.py#L16-32 hope this helps :-)

I think you might be misunderstanding.  VCS Dependencies _MUST EXECUTE_ the `setup.py` of the vcs repo in question.  That in turn installs some stuff.  Whether that stuff also winds up in your lockfile depends upon you specifying the `--editable` flag.  

We can't _not_ install dependencies here, so we can just leave that one alone.

> VCS repo dependencies install their dependencies, which can override the dependencies specified in Pipfile.lock.

No, they don't override the lockfile if you include the editable flag so that their dependencies land in the lockfile.  That's why we have a warning about this. If you leave this off, then yeah, the dependencies are getting installed either way, so I guess you're rolling the dice.

> Based on my investigation, this is not a resolution order issue, it's a race condition during parallel installation 

So it's not an issue with the order in which things are resolved => installed, it's just a race condition related to the order in which things are resolved and installed.

Yeah, I've encountered this before, but I don't know that there's an easy solution to it. The extension and the `setup_requires` both need to be passed to the `setup` function, so `Extension` needs to be pulled in *before* `setup` is executed.

In Rust extensions, I've seen [this done](https://github.com/PyO3/pyo3/blob/master/examples/word-count/setup.py#L6), which basically solves the problem.

Another possible option would be to add some mechanism for deferred execution, so the `import` statements can be nested in commands run only *after* `setup_requires` has installed the missing modules.

I was under the impression that the one true solution to this is PEP 518, and setup-time requirements will be resolved from `pyproject.toml`.

Sorry, I was wrong. I've just refreshed my memories on [!!merge type](http://yaml.org/type/merge.html). This type is implemented as a hack on storeMappingPair-level (there was reasons to) and we need this to be slightly changed to handle both unique key policy and !!merge type behaviour. So, the rules are:

1. If target mapping contains keys `a` and `b` BEFORE `<< : ...` line, then we skip these keys in merge sources.
2. If there are several merge sources (like `<< : [ *CENTER, *BIG ]`), then they override keys of each other from left to right.
3. AFTER `<< : ...` line we must be able to override keys from merged mappings, but NOT keys that were in the mapping before `<< : ...`.

To implement this we need to keep the list of keys merged from other mappings to distinct them from "normal" keys. I think we'll have to keep this list at readBlockMapping/readFlowSequence-level.

A good reference on the topic: http://www.cl.cam.ac.uk/~mgk25/unicode.html#term picked up from iTerm2 source code.

From ANSI escape code:
> In 8-bit environments, these two character sequences can be merged into single byte, using the 0x80–0x9F control character range.[13]:5.3.b Devices that support just ASCII (7-bit bytes) recognize only the two-character sequence. The same is true for devices that support 8-bit bytes but use the 0x80–0x9F control character range for other purposes. On terminals that use UTF-8 encoding, both forms take 2 bytes (CSI in UTF-8 is 0xC2, 0x9B)[discuss] but the ESC[ sequence is clearer.


And from C0 and C1 control codes:
> Unicode sets aside 65 code points for compatibility with ISO/IEC 2022. The Unicode control characters cover U+0000—U+001F (C0 controls), U+007F (delete), and U+0080—U+009F (C1 controls). Unicode only specifies semantics for U+001C—U+001F, U+0009—U+000D, and U+0085. The rest of the control characters are transparent to Unicode and their meanings are left to higher-level protocols. The C1 characters in Unicode require 2 bytes to be encoded in UTF-8 (for instance CSI at U+009B is encoded as the bytes 0xC2, 0x9B in UTF-8). Thus the corresponding control functions are more commonly accessed using the equivalent two byte escape sequence intended for use with systems that have only 7-bit bytes.

Very good point about vendoring six. I'd actually be in favor of that since it's just one file to add. We may even want to write our own compatibility layer by stealing just the bits that we use from six as there aren't that many.

For this PR I took a conservative approach so as not to modify existing behavior. Basically what I did was use `2to3` to identify all the places that need to be updated, then used six to make these lines version-agnostic. (I also replaced all uses of `sys.version_info` with `six.PY2` / `six.PY3` for consistency.)

If you use `2to3` on the code from this branch, you'd only get the following changes:

- removing `from __future__ import ...` lines. This is not needed as these imports are just a no-op with Python 3

- removing the `u` prefix from the string literals that use it. This is not needed either -- while the prefix was initially removed in Python 3, it was re-introduced in 3.3 for compatibility (it's a no-op as well). We don't support 3.0-3.2 so this is not an issue.

- a few places where Python 2-specific symbols are used (e.g. `unicode`), but those lines are in an `if six.PY2` block or equivalent

I'm fairly sure we could do a lot of improvements to string handling and start using Unicode ~everywhere, but we'd need to be careful about that and maybe release with a major version bump, so I left that part out for now.

> Why do we care about python bindings in a C++ library used inside nupic.core?

I'm assuming the end goal is to remove the current sparse libraries in nupic.core and replace them with external C++ libraries. Currently the C++ sparse libraries in nupic.core are used directly in our Python code to implement the spatial pooler, KNN classifier, and elsewhere. 

An alternative path is to replace those python uses with a standard Python sparse library, such as scipy (Sagan proposed we do this). If we can do that everywhere without losing significant performance then we don't need python bindings for linear algebra C++ libraries in nupic.core.  Python developers will also then be using libraries that are familiar to them instead of some C++ library.

**1.** Regarding "subject", we have talked about it independently with @jalavik and @Kennethhole.  This was added via INSPIRE search branch, but IMO it should be removed.  If a special treatment for "subject" is needed, then it is better achieved via dedicated tokeniser, like for author field.  Actually, @Kennethhole has a client that uses an index called "subject" with another default tokeniser...  So this creates a problem. `search_unit()` should not have hard-coded index names, we are actually going to remove it for v1.2.1.

**2.** Regarding "e" matching type, the situation is kind of subtle.  Users are usually coming via high-level search APIs such as "search_pattern()" etc.  These call tokeniser "create_basic_search_units()" where matching type "e" can be used, signifying "exact match".  When you look at the code of "create_basic_search_units()", when "e" is passed on the input, the function changes the unit to "a" (=access).  Hence "search_unit()" later sees only "a" usually; it never sees "e".
Hence we should not really need to catch it there.

**TL;DR** The matching type "e" is used in higher-level search APIs, and is transformed to "a" before the lower-level APIs like "search_unit()" are called.  (The same happens for "p" matching type, for example.) 

Hence question: in which usage scenario do you see "search_unit(p=..., f=..., m='e')" being called? This could be an issue of changing the client call... in which case we don't need to change anything in the search code, but just adapt the client.  

Basically, we have two options: either (1) simply better document which "m" values are expected at each search level step; or (2) enrich search_unit() to accept all the other matching types too, i.e. not only "e" but also "p" and friends.

@jirikuncar In this case the problem is not for parsing but for generating. Both `Z` and `+HH:MM` formats are valid ISO 8601 formats. Python's `datetime.isoformat` generates only the second one. Thus the only solution I see to generate a `Z` is to provide a format string, which might contain a typo if it is used in many places. That's why I would rather use `datetime.isoformat` even if it uses more bandwidth.

I have trouble following what is happening in this code (not the pull request per se, but the catalog code in general), but the direction seems good. I agree with the detailed code quality remarks by Tres and the notes by Hanno.

What Tres said.

The index is optimized to only store the less common value, thereby decreasing its size. Usually the distribution of the true/false values is fairly stable, so this isn't a problem, e.g. the ratio between folders and non-folders tends to stay the same. If the ratio changes the index will wait until the less common value represents at least 60% of all items and than does an inplace migration. The 60% is meant to protect against ratios, which are almost 50/50 and would cause constant switching if one or the other value becomes less frequent.

Unfortunately Zope has no general background task framework, where this kind of task could be offloaded to. So it does this as part of a user request and that request can suddenly take minutes on large sites. But it should be really rare for this to happen at all.

In Hinton's slides, z is a score vector and to get a distribution over labels, you take y = softmax(z). In my blog post, y the distribution vector, not the score vector.

So, to change the temperature of z, you simply divide by T - no arguments there. However, to change the temperature when you already have y, you have take it to the power of 1/T and then re-normalize.

It follows from the fact that exp(z/T) = exp(z)^(1/T). Makes sense?

Yes, ubsan reports errors on x86_64 and we used it to check "portable" version.
But reporting unaligned reads by ubsan on x86_64 is done intentionally, so developers could verify their product without compiling for / running on specific hardware that can't to unaligned reads.

FWIW, cppcheck picks up a couple minor issues, too:

```
[brotli/enc/block_splitter_inc.h:280] -> [brotli/enc/block_splitter_inc.h:281]: (style) Variable 'pairs' is reassigned a value before the old one has been used.
[brotli/enc/write_bits.h:80]: (warning) %d in format string (no. 1) requires 'int' but the argument type is 'size_t {aka unsigned long}'.
```

To fix the second one, you could cast to int, or if you want to really use size_t you can do something like this (stolen from https://github.com/nemequ/munit/blob/master/munit.h#L56):

```c
#if !defined(_WIN32)
  #define BROTLI_SIZE_SPECIFIER "zu"
#elif defined(_M_X64) || defined(__amd64__)
  #define BROTLI_SIZE_SPECIFIER "I64u"
#else
  #define BROTLI_SIZE_SPECIFIER "u"
#endif
```

Thanks for your contribution. While I'm open to style changes, it's important that we remain stylistically consistent across the project.

For now we're following the internal Google Python style guide. The most notable difference from the [external one](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html) is that it permits capital-letter FunctionNames. While I would like to switch to a public style guide, it's important that we maintain consistency within the project and also that we maintain the bare-minimum level of backwards compatibility (i.e. exposing a function named `Fire`).

Let's hold off on any widespread style changes until I figure out how to release the pylintrc that we're using internally.

Just to close the case with some relevant info. There was some debugging that we've done with H2O and a custom patch was developed (released with Sparkling Water 1.5.16). It enables to use a new parameter `spark.ext.h2o.node.network.mask` to specify a mask for internal IPs.

Here's a way to run the tool so that it works:
```
spark-submit \
--class water.SparklingWaterDriver \
--master yarn-client \
--num-executors 3 \
--driver-memory 4g \
--executor-memory 2g \
--executor-cores 1 \
--conf "spark.scheduler.minRegisteredResourcesRatio=1" \
--conf "spark.ext.h2o.topology.change.listener.enabled=false" \
--conf "spark.ext.h2o.node.network.mask=<IP_NUMBER>/<MASK>" \
--conf "spark.ext.h2o.fail.on.unsupported.spark.param=false" \
/opt/sparkling-water/sparkling-water-1.5.16/assembly/build/libs/*.jar
```
e.g. `"spark.ext.h2o.node.network.mask=10.0.00/24"`

> I guess by T here, you don't mean the trace T = Ixx + Iyy (Kaiser's definition), but rather the full 
> second moment matrix (sometimes denoted Q).

Oops.  Yes.  (And here I was talking about notational confusion!)  I meant the full matrix.

Naming: how about ```calculateMomentRadius``` and having it return both the determinant and trace radius?   I would imagine the expensive part of the calculation is drawing the image, not doing the calculation, so it seems worth doing both.  Or, if I'm wrong, have a kwarg for the type of radius with 'trace' and 'determinant' options.

Yes, in GREAT3 we used the trace, but it does give ellipticity-dependent selection effects.  Thinking beyond the attempt to mimic what GREAT3 did, I would be more likely to use a determinant radius than a trace radius in future.

I'm pretty sure the only other place where we would want to use this class is the PowerSpectrum interpolation (as Rachel mentioned above).  I toyed with implementing LookupTable2D there as part of the work in PR #911.  (I did reorganize the code there significantly to use InterpolatedImage rather than SBInterpolatedImage and generally smooth out the code flow.)

The problem is that it uses Laczos interpolation and has pretty strong caveats that linear and cubic are not accurate enough, adding significant spurious power to the correlation function.  So I think in order to make the switch there, we'd need to implement Lanczos interpolation in LookupTable2D, which is a rather big job.

So we can keep this issue open if people are interested in doing that work, but I've changed the name to be more descriptive of what the job actually is.

I've been thinking this over... when you posted your first message i thought OK, just convolve with an Airy and that kills the large-k modes and the composite object has a totally sensible `max_k`.  Even just a pixel response will help, though not as much as an Airy.  So I feel like you're fine with warning if the delta would be >some threshold value in the encircled flux.  If you want to be adventurous you could also have a kwarg specifying how strict to be, i.e., if `strict=True` then getting into that regime raises an exception while if `strict=False` it's just a warning, or something like that.

Actually, this points out that `__repr__` and `__eq__` are also incorrect currently.  `eval(repr(rw))` currently returns a new realization with different points.  And `__eq__` erroneously declares them to be equal.

So along the way when you're working on this, you should add the test:
```
do_pickle(conv, lambda x: x.drawImage())
```
which currently fails.  The regular pickle test uses `__eq__` which wrongly thinks the two objects are equivalent.

I think `__eq__` should check that the positions of the points are equal.  And for  `__repr__`, I can think of two options.
1. You can use a constructor argument that explicitly lists the point positions rather than generate new ones.
2. You can save the rng state (with `rng.duplicate()`) before generating the point positions and then pass that as the random number generator to use in `__repr__`.

Either of these would also facilitate the `shear` override to preserve the point positions.  The second one would likely be easier, since you can rely on the shoot function still to generate the sheared positions.  In option 1, you would probably need to move the points by hand.

> With static discovery the only we can do, is to try INITIAL list of etcd nodes provided in the config. Unfortunately there is no guaranty that there is some process (or somebody) keeping  all Patroni config files up to date and with really high chance etcd nodes from the config don't exists anymore and therefore Patroni will never reconnect to etcd...

Currently, if you have only one host used for initial discovery, you have to update the configuration file only when that host is moved. If you specify more than one host there, you'll have to update the configuration file when each of those hosts specified in the configuration file is moved (otherwise, you'll eventually end up with a list of non-existing hosts). In other words, you still need a sync process, but you'll end up doing more work if you sync multiple (presumably all) hosts instead of one. I don't really see any benefits to that.

Hi,

Actually your logs contain the reason why pg_basebackup has failed:
```
Apr 29 18:30:23 slave01 patroni[30015]: pg_basebackup: child process exited with error 1
```

`pg_basebackup: child process exited with error 1` -- this is the error message printed by pg_basebackup

By default Patroni starts pg_basebackup with `-X stream` arguments. pg_basebackup in this case spawns additional process which receives wal stream and writes it into pg_xlog directory.

For some unknown reason this subprocess exists earlier than expected (and with non zero exit code) what causes entire pg_basebackup to fail...

Patroni itself doesn't know anything about tablespaces and therefore can't clean them up before the next pg_basebackup attempt. Likely there it is possible to configure Patroni to use other methods to build relicas rather than default pg_basebackup: http://patroni.readthedocs.io/en/latest/replica_bootstrap.html#building-replicas

I would recommend you to wrap pg_basebackup into a shell script and inside this script clean up tablespaces before calling pg_basebackup. Also if you archiving wals it make sense to avoid streaming wals from pg_basebackup (don't specify -X stream).

Here you can also grab some ideas: https://github.com/zalando/spilo/blob/master/postgres-appliance/basebackup.sh
