I experimented a little more with the SendinBlue transactional APIs, and found a few things:
* You can set an X-Mailin-custom header to an arbitrary string, and it will be passed to webhooks. This would be a good way to implement Anymail's [metadata](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/sending/anymail_additions/#anymail.message.AnymailMessage.metadata) extension, by passing it as JSON ([example](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/blob/v1.4/anymail/backends/mailjet.py#L224-L226)). The exact capitalization of the header name seems to matter, and SiB's docs aren't always consistent. Lowercase "custom" is the one that worked for me. (Ditto X-Mailin-tag, btw. X-Mailin-Tag with a capital T doesn't get passed to webhooks.)
* Attachment content-type is determined from the filename extension. (And trying to send an attachment without a name or a name without an extension generates a 400 response with an "Unsupported file format" message. I don't think the code needs to do anything special for this, but it's worth noting in the docs.)
* SendinBlue has a bunch of limitations that might not be expected coming from other ESPs (e.g., can't send inline images, can't specify display-names when sending a template, etc.). I'll add inline notes for the other ones I found. In all these cases, the payload needs to call `self.unsupported_feature("...")`. (Issuing errors on unsupported features is important for users trying to switch ESPs, in case they're counting on something that won't work.)

Let me know if you want to take a crack at those, otherwise I can fill them in.

Also, you're the first person other than me to fully implement a new backend for Anymail, so a huge thanks! I've learned a lot from this about places I should improve the internal documentation and porting notes, and I really appreciate the effort you put into powering through what's there now.

The only thing preventing this from working is the deleted default constructor: the eigen code assumes the type is default constructible, and is essentially doing:  ``type_caster<YourType> converter; /* various stuff gets checked */; converter.load(o, false);` where `o` is a python object.  The failure is in the first statement, because internally the type caster has a default-constructed `YourType value;` member.

To work around this, you'd have to duplicate the regular, dense matrix `type_caster` from eigen.h with a specialization for your class, but with a `load` that stores the internal instance in some other way (for example, inside a `std::unique_ptr<VectorXdConstSize>`, or an `std::optional` if under C++17) and extracts the instance in the appropriate way in the `operator Type` implementations.

(On a side note, with your code I think `void foo(Ref<VectorXd> v) { v.resize(5); }` *does* allow resizing).

Hope you don't mind me pushing directly here, but your simplification gave me an idea to take it further still. I wasn't sure about it, had to write it out completely and test it. Looks like it works. 

Now,  `method_adaptor` just does a simple cast from `Return (Source::*)(Args...)` to `Return (Target::*)(Args...)`. Because it's an implicit cast, the compiler will only allow compatible `Source` -> `Target` member function conversions, like `Base` -> `Derived`, but `Class` -> `UnrelatedClass` will result in a compile-time error.

> just completely getting rid of the reference to the object from the Python side, if that's possible at all.

Unfortunately, it isn't: we can't do anything about references to the instance held elsewhere.  At best you could replace the pointer with something else, but then the question is what "something else" should be--and since it has to be some sort of object that you're going to have to duplicate, you might as well just consume the duplicate.  (I suppose you could add some sort of error detection for trying to use an instance that is already cleared, but that's going a bit far into esoteric territory).

I am reviewing this PR.

Also a general comment @palango: Do we really need to specify that constructors return `None` everywhere? I find it completely redundant and I would not want to have to write the obvious for every constructor I write.

What I find a little weird with the single object manipulation is the redundancy of some of the fields depending on the state of the  channel and on the endpoint (like @konradkonrad stated). So the `asset_address, partner_address` and `channel_address` are mutually exclusive when the channel is existing.
`deposit` is only relevant when you either create the channel or the channel is in it's `opened` state.
`settle` and `close`-actions are only defined by the `status` and `channel_address` fields.

Say we have a `channel_state` and a read-only `channel` object (like @konradkonrad suggested):
We would have to think about how to handle the redundancy in the `channel_state`. 
I'd say we have to force that either `asset_address, partner_address` or `channel_address` is provided. And since we cannot use `channel_address` for the creation of a channel, it should be excluded from the object completely.

With the approach of multiple endpoints, there is also the intersection of 'arguments' (as object fields) and endpoint-arguments that I was talking about earlier. If we take precedence of the endpoint-arguments, then the relevant object-fields change again depending on the endpoint.

So since the fields are completely dynamic depending on state and endpoint, it would introduce a lot of confusing logic in creating and checking the requests.
The only way I see the RFC-approach would be to **exclusively** allow a `PUT /api/<version>/channels`
endpoint and exclude the `channel_address` from the `channel_state`-object to get rid of the mutually exclusive fields.

Otherwise I think we should go completely endpoint-defined, and don't use object's for state updates at all, which sounds a little hacky. But then again for transfers, objects would be handy because they have a uniform interface and don't depend on external state.

I cannot really decide which way is best, but I get the feeling that the object-approach is very uniform, but harder to explain to the end-user? Still, I think I like it better.

What I was wondering:

- Do we make the assumption, that the client always polls the full list of `channel`s on connect and then listens on the events permanently? Then it would be easy for him to construct the desired state-transition objects for a given channel. 
The only scenario where you would try to do a state-change without knowing the previous state would be to try to `close` a channel as an emergency measure?

- What do we actually gain from layered/multiple endpoints? I mean for 'browsing' for information it makes sense. But maybe we overestimate the gain of a RESTful API, since we don't really browse a database but rather do a lot of state updates, which would be best defined by the arguments of the function/method itself?

1. Given a remote function

    ```python
    @ray.remote
    def f():

    ```

We can't just get rid of the surrounding quotes and do @bastimeyer's command `streamlink -p mpv -a "--title=\"{title}\" {filename}" URL best` because we replaced single quotes with double quotes. You'd have to glue the strings together like `streamlink -p mpv -a '--title='"'"'{title}'"'"' {filename}' URL best` which is not very desirable.

and as @wlerin points out, it's not very possible to escape the string in Windows at all.

So, this commit takes the entire title and puts it into shlex_quote/list2cmdline instead of just the _get_title() part. Unfortunately, I've had to introduce some unique syntax in order to identify the start and end of the user defined title, since media players have different ways of parsing a user defined title from the command line.

-----

Now titles can be used in two ways:

`streamlink https://www.twitch.tv/coolstreamer source -p mpv -a '--title={title} {filename}'` if you're just passing _get_title() along

`streamlink https://www.twitch.tv/coolstreamer source -p mpv -a '--title=@@foo {title} bar@@ {filename}'` if you'd like to include {title} in some longer custom title.

First off, I'd like to state that Config *does* reload JSON data from disk when reloaded. Once config detects that all driver objects referencing a particular cog's data have been garbage collected it dumps the JSON cache and on next load will load that data from disk. However, because of a lot of technical reasons, it is *possible* that the new cog object is instantiated before the old one can be garbage collected. This is a race condition and unintentional but likely unavoidable consequence. Simply using `!unload` and waiting a second or two before using `!load` again should give you the behavior you want.

With that being said, this is not truly a bug because you are directly modifying files that should be entirely controlled by config and the config driver. As has been said above, use config functions where possible and for the future we may be able to add file change detection for the JSON driver, although that is not near the top of our current priorities. If you use Mongo you will never experience this issue and, in fact, will not even need to reload.

I'm not sure having another special-purpose event is the right path in terms of ease of use and clear API.

A counter example: it was trivial to make a event supporting level and edge trigger with any polarity and a simple property interface for the value (sorry, impl omitted):

```
class BoolEvent:
    """Boolean offering the ability to wait for a value or transition."""

    def __init__(self, value=False): ...

    @property
    def value(self): ...

    @value.setter
    def value(self, x): ...

    async def wait_value(self, x=True):
        """Wait until given value."""
        ...

    async def wait_transition(self, x=True):
        """Wait until transition to given value."""
        ...
```

Using a property and not having the user bother about set/clear/get is very nice, as is making level and edge trigger very clear in the api and supporting all use cases an once.

@belm0 FWIW I have this working quite well under a minimal actor model [here](https://github.com/tgoodlet/tractor/tree/draft_readme). Though no larger uses (yet) to prove these concepts can scale, the adherence to `trionic` principles is demonstrating much easier to understand failure modes so far. `tractor` also uses [`multiprocessing`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/multiprocessing.html) so there is no GIL constraint and the *thread-local state* is implicit.

I personally am **very** interesting to see how `trionic` concepts can adapted in new languages and threading models. Given this recent project, I'm starting to think the lifetime and cancellation constraints can be applied to distributed processes on a much grander scale (under certain caveats) with good results.

@ncoghlan: OK, but... that's a circular argument. My proposal only breaks substitutability of subexpressions if we decide that in `await foo(1, 2, 3)`, the `foo(1, 2, 3)` part is a subexpression. But that's exactly the point under discussion.

Analogy: In modern Python, this:
```python
foo(1, 2, 3)
```
does not mean the same thing as:
```python
extracted = 1, 2, 3
foo(extracted)
```
It didn't have to be this way – in fact, once upon a time these *were* equivalent! If you search [Misc/HISTORY](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/python/cpython/master/Misc/HISTORY) for "sleepless night" you can see Guido agonizing about whether to change it. But the current behavior is fine and doesn't violate your dictum (even though it looks like it should), because we decided to define `...(..., ..., ...)` as being a single piece of syntax, rather than being composed out of call parentheses `...(...)` plus tuple constructor commas `..., ..., ...`. In fact it's obvious now that this change made the language better.

My contention is that Python would also be a better language – easier to understand, teach, use, etc. – if we were to decide that in the expression `await foo(1, 2, 3)`, the `await ...(..., ..., ...)` part was defined as a single piece of syntax, instead of being composed out of `await ...` plus call parentheses `...(..., ..., ...)`. It's not a trivial point – the [trio tutorial](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial.html) adopts this stance when teaching async/await, and I had multiple people seeking me out at PyCon specifically to thank me for that tutorial, saying it was the only async/await explanation they'd ever understood. And the tutorial's explanation isn't oversimplified at all; it really teaches you everything you need to know to write arbitrary programs using trio. Yet it's still 2x longer than it needs to be, because literally half of the explanation is about how to cope with the problems caused by `foo(1, 2, 3)` being a subexpression. (See [slide 34](https://vorpus.org/%7Enjs/misc/trio-language-summit-2017.pdf).)

You can certainly disagree with my claim about how Python should work, or agree with it but feel that it's too late to do anything. But you can't say that that a proposal to change the definition of subexpressions is bad because it violates substitutability of subexpressions :-)

If it's useful, here is an example of a `Config` that gets (almost) everything under a working directory, `wd`. The relevant overrides are called out. The folder `.ipython` remains in `$HOME` and an empty directory `$HOME/parsl_scripts` is still created. 

The code below uses local modifications to work around an issue I reported [here](https://github.com/Parsl/libsubmit/issues/74) and a launcher I have submitted a PR for [here](https://github.com/Parsl/libsubmit/pull/73).


```
blocks = 2
nodes = 2
ppn = 32

wd = os.getenv("HOME") + '/hello_parsl/'
os.makedirs(wd, exist_ok = True)

ipp_config = Config(
    run_dir = wd, # Override 1
    retries = 0,
    executors = [
        IPyParallelExecutor(
            label="IPPHello",
            engine_dir = wd, #Override 2
            engine_file = wd + '/ipcontroller-engine.json', #Override 3
            provider = TorqueProvider(
                launcher = MpiExecLauncher(),
                init_blocks = blocks,
                max_blocks = blocks,
                min_blocks = 0,
                nodes_per_block = nodes,
                tasks_per_node = ppn,
                walltime = "00:02:00",
                script_dir = wd + 'provider_scripts', # Override 4
                channel = SSHChannel(hostname='hostname',
                                     username=os.getenv("USER"),
                                     script_dir = wd + '/channel_scripts'), # Override 5
                overrides = f'''
# Override 6
cd {wd}
                '''
            )
        )
    ]
)

parsl.load(ipp_config)
```

So, I was quite busy the last weeks, but I found the time to make all the tests turn green. I had to adapt some so they match PyEcore specificity. They also help me fixing some bugs.

Now that I have two textX version: the original one, and one with PyEcore, it will be easier to see how to properly add a kind of "swtich" to enable or disable PyEcore in textX. It should not be so hard, but here is one modification that is more complex than the others: the cross-ref elements resolution.

In the original version, during the model building, each time a cross-ref towards an other element is found, a `CrossRef` element is created and added in the model graph. Later, the whole graph is scanned and each time a `CrossRef` is found, the reference is resolved and the `CrossRef` is replaced by the resolved element. 

With the PyEcore integration, this cannot be done as collections are typed, so the placeholder cannot be added like this. Instead of storing the `CrossRef` in the model and resolving it later during the second model graph traversal, I store them as parser instance attribute. At the end, the same mecanism for resolving is used, but this time, I only iterate over the stored `CrossRef`. This cuts the recursive algorithm, but between the first and the second pass, it puts the model in a 'not-complete' state. Is the recursive version without the instance attribute better for textX? Or would the store+iterative version work? 

If this modification is not right for the original textX, I will create two different methods for the parser. The new one will be called if the PyEcoree support is enabled.

```Python
import tensorflow as tf
import tensorlayer as tl

with tf.device('/cpu:0'):
    def inference():
        x = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, shape=[None, 784], name='x')
        network = tl.layers.InputLayer(x, name='input')
        network = tl.layers.DropoutLayer(network, keep=0.8, name='drop1')
        network = tl.layers.DenseLayer(network, n_units=800, act=tf.nn.relu, name='relu1')
        network = tl.layers.DropoutLayer(network, keep=0.5, name='drop2')
        network = tl.layers.DenseLayer(network, n_units=800, act=tf.nn.relu, name='relu2')
        network = tl.layers.DropoutLayer(network, keep=0.5, name='drop3')
        network = tl.layers.DenseLayer(network, n_units=10, act=tf.identity, name='output')
        return network


if __name__ == '__main__':
    with tf.device('/gpu:0'):
        network = inference()
        network.print_layers()
        sess = tf.Session(config=tf.ConfigProto(
                allow_soft_placement=True,
                log_device_placement=True))
        tl.layers.initialize_global_variables(sess)
```

`redirect_uri` management in oauthlib for authorization code is as below:

Browser's request response_type=code:
----
See RFC's section:

    redirect_uri
         OPTIONAL


Implementation (pseudo code):

    if redirect_uri in request:
        validate_redirect_uri(request.redirect_uri)
    else
        get_default_redirect_uri()

Client's request grant_type=authorization_code:
-------
See RFC's section: 

    redirect_uri
         REQUIRED, if the "redirect_uri" parameter was included in the
         authorization request as described in Section 4.1.1, and their
         values MUST be identical.


**Current** implementation (oauthlib 2.x) 

    if redirect_uri in request:
        confirm_redirect_uri(request.redirect_uri)
    else
        confirm_redirect_uri(None)


**Proposed** implementation (oauthlib 3.x) 

    if redirect_uri in request:
        confirm_redirect_uri(request.redirect_uri)
    else
        confirm_redirect_uri(get_default_redirect_uri())

What do you think ? It will call `get_default_redirect_uri()` also in the `grant_type=authorization_code` request, but it doesn't break the RFC because at the end, the implementation will verify that `their values MUST be identical`.
